dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 95-year-old female with potential assault and altered mental status.
Good morning, I'm Medic Taylor with the rescue squad. Can you tell me why you called 911 today?
She has been unresponsive, unable to speak or follow directions since last night. We checked on her earlier and called EMS after consulting the on-call doctor.
She had some kind of disagreement with her husband the night before and has been confused this morning.
The patient's chief complaint is altered mental status. She is disoriented and incoherently babbles without being able to answer questions.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 12: eye 4, verbal 3, motor 5.
She is disoriented and a/ox0/4, showing confusion and no coherent verbal communication.
Pupils are equal, round, reactive to light, at 2mm bilaterally, and tracking is normal.
On EKG, she shows sinus rhythm with occasional premature atrial contractions.
Blood pressure readings include 187 over 68, later 147 over 78, and a final reading of 193 over 91.
Pulse is 63 initially, then elevates to 92, and later drops to 79.
Initial respiratory rate was 39, then drops to 12, indicating significant variability.
Oxygen saturation drops to 87% at first but improves to 96% after administering oxygen, and then later shows 95% on subsequent monitoring.
Glucose level was 196 mg/dL initially; no further readings were collected later.
Both arms and legs show spontaneous movement, but grip strength is weak and equal bilaterally. No pallor noted.
Performing capnography for continuous evaluation and monitoring.
Placing an IV in the right antecubital with an 18-gauge catheter.
Administering oxygen at 3 L per minute via nasal cannula.
Vitals now at pulse 92, BP 147 over 78, RR 12, and spO2 of 96.
Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, hyperlipidemia, anxiety, GERD, osteoarthritis, and osteoporosis.
Continuous cardiac monitoring via 4-lead and two 12-lead ECGs show sinus rhythm with occasional PACs and possible anterior infarct.
Hospital was notified with a pre-arrival report. The patient's daughter is on scene and will meet us at the hospital.
Positioning the patient in semi-Fowler's with shoulder and lap harnesses for transport.
Patient exhibited signs of mild hypoxia with initial spO2 of 87%, which improved with supplemental oxygen.
The patient showed no pain at any point during the assessment or during transport.
A second 12-lead EKG shows significant findings, including abnormal left axis deviation and minimal ST depression.
The patient will be transported directly for a CT scan at the hospital.
Confirming semi-Fowler's position for transport due to initial hypoxia and altered mental status.
Skin is warm, dry, and of normal color with no signs of trauma or cyanosis noted.
Normocephalic head, no deformities, lacerations, or signs of trauma noted during secondary assessment.
Chest rise and fall appear appropriate with equal bilateral breath sounds and no signs of chest wall trauma.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and without rigidity.
No jugular venous distention or nuchal rigidity detected during the neck assessment.
HEENT exam shows symmetrical face and clear nasal passages with no drainage or abnormalities.
Arms and legs both show equal spontaneous movement with no deformities or edema noted.
Patient continues to show altered mental status, not following commands or answering questions.
Pre-hospital report sent to hospital with all pertinent findings including EKG results and IV placement.
Handing off patient care to hospital staff as per protocol with daughter and daughter's contact information provided.
Patient's daughter confirms medical history, including medications and allergies on file, with no additions.
EKG trends consistently show occasional PACs with no change in overall rhythm or acuity prior to hospital arrival.
Capnography remains normal with consistent end-tidal CO₂ levels observed throughout transport.
Final vitals prior to hospital entry are pulse 79, BP 193/91, RR 12, and spO₂ 95% on 3 L O₂.
Patient is secured for transport with shoulder harness and lap restraints, and transport is initiated.
No improvement in mental status; patient continues to be incoherent and unaware of surroundings.
IV remains patent and flushable with no signs of infiltration or complications.
Oxygen delivery via nasal cannula continues to maintain adequate spO₂ levels.
Transferring patient to stretcher within the hospital with continued semi-Fowler's position maintained.
Vitals repeated at final hospital arrival: pulse 79, BP 193/91, RR 12, spO₂ 95%.
Ongoing EKG and capnography monitoring is continued within the hospital upon transfer.
Allergies noted include penicillin and pravastatin, but no reaction observed during treatment.
No obvious signs of trauma or injury present on physical examination.
Patient has baseline diagnosis of anxiety, GERD, and osteoarthritis with normal cognitive function until this morning.
Continual mental status assessment pre-hospital confirms no improvement from a/ox0/4 to a/ox1/3 or higher.
No signs of periorbital ecchymosis or external trauma to ears or nose.
Oral cavity shows clean, intact mucosa, no gross bleeding or obstruction.
No loose dentures or signs of aspiration risk detected during brief oral examination.
Pre-transport check is complete, with all interventions in place and vitals stable for transport.
In-transit EKG monitoring continues to show occasional PACs.
No new signs or symptoms develop during transport, including no changes in rhythm or mentation.
Destination hospital is acknowledging patient on route with CT preparation confirmed.
Final hand-off to hospital performed with patient triaged directly for CT due to high clinical suspicion of acute neurological changes.
Notes completed for all encounters, including medical history, vital signs, EKG results, and patient behaviors in transit.
Transfer of care is complete with hospital nurse accepting patient and confirming intent to proceed with CT imaging.
Arrival report completed with full pre-hospital care summary, including IV, EKGs, and oxygen administered.
Submission of all medical reports has been scheduled as digitized per standing orders and EMD guidelines.
Attempt to repeat glucose is waived due to logistical challenges and urgent care required at hospital.
Arrival at destination hospital is announced, and patient is wheeled inside with EMS team in tow.
A second 12-lead EKG is performed upon hospital entry with no findings resulting in protocol deviation.
Medical EMS protocols completed with protocol number logged and filed with standard procedure.
Final takeown of care is accepted by hospital with no further field interventions required.
Skin condition is normal with turgor intact and no rashes or temperature changes detected.
Bilateral breath sounds are equal and clear with no wheezes or crackles noted during full assessment.
Patient is clean, well-dressed, and presented with no unusual findings.
GCS reconfirmed as 12 before transport, with no change in mental status noted.
Continued monitoring reveals an abnormal left axis deviation and minimal ST depression.
Patient remains within acceptable saturation levels with capnography confirming normal CO₂ levels.
Records handed to receiving hospital nurse with full summary of IV, EKG, and oxygen administered.
Emergency nurse acknowledges records and confirms pt as complete for CT imaging and follow-up.
EMS crew departs hospital after confirming pt care acceptance and completion of field report.
EMS crew initiates vehicle and equipment decontamination, logs next units for imminent call.
Crew begins prep for next call, checking vitals and equipment to ensure all ready.
Blood pressure increase at later time may be due to compensatory actions.
All EKGs and capnography printed and secured with patient for hospital record completion.
Baseline mental status of the pt is fully alert and conversant, per caregiver and daughter statements.
Nurse affirms patient was alert pre-illness and is now disoriented.
Hospital confirms CT scan will address acute onset neurological deficits.
No capnography errors observed; waveforms remain clear throughout the transport period.
All vitals are recorded at appropriate times and categorized for hospital use.
